summary: Joel documents his quest to connect a professional studio microphone to a standard computer sound card for better audio quality in his CityDesk online demos. He details the exact equipment chain needed: a Shure SM58 microphone, a preamp to boost the signal to line level, and the right combination of cables and adapters. He notes that the music store salesperson confidently sold him the wrong setup, missing the preamp entirely. The post serves as a practical guide for others attempting the same thing, and includes before/after audio samples to demonstrate the improvement.
key_points: A professional microphone like the Shure SM58 cannot be plugged directly into a computer sound card — it requires a preamp to boost the signal to line level.
Professional music store staff may sound confident but give incomplete or wrong advice about connecting pro audio gear to consumer computer hardware.
Professional music stores refuse to stock 1/8-inch jack cables, creating an annoying gap between pro audio and consumer computer equipment.
The total setup requires four components: microphone, desk stand, mic cable, preamp, and an adapter cable chain to reach the sound card's mini jack.
money_quotes: The stoner DJ sales dude sounded very confident but he didn't tell me that I needed a preamp, and he gave me the wrong kind of cables.
For some reason the professional music stores like Sam Ash and Zzounds think it is beneath their dignity to stock any parts with 1/8″ jacks, but that is what your sound card needs.
key_insight: Bridging the gap between professional audio equipment and consumer computer hardware requires specific knowledge about signal levels and connectors that even music store staff may not have.
